scenarios.title: Bag Weight Label
scenarios.context: A student has one school bag with a weight written on a scale.
scenarios.prompt: What single piece of information would you store so the computer can remember the bag weight?
scenarios.sampleReasoning: I only need to remember the bag weight, so I would store it with a clear name.

scenarios.title: Rainy Day Choice
scenarios.context: Before leaving home, a learner checks whether it is raining.
scenarios.prompt: What small decision rule would help the learner decide whether to carry an umbrella?
scenarios.sampleReasoning: If it is raining, carry an umbrella. Otherwise, leave it at home.

scenarios.title: Two Snack Prices
scenarios.context: A learner buys one samosa and one juice from the canteen.
scenarios.prompt: How would you figure out the total cost using just the two prices?
scenarios.sampleReasoning: I would keep the two prices separately and add them to get the total.

scenarios.title: Greeting by Name
scenarios.context: A classroom screen should greet one learner by name.
scenarios.prompt: What text should the computer remember, and how should it combine that text into a greeting?
scenarios.sampleReasoning: The computer should store the learner name and place it inside a greeting sentence.

scenarios.title: Pass Mark Check
scenarios.context: A learner receives one test score and wants to know whether it is at least the pass mark.
scenarios.prompt: What comparison would decide if the learner passed?
scenarios.sampleReasoning: I would compare the score with the pass mark and decide pass if it is high enough.

scenarios.title: Pocket Money Left
scenarios.context: A learner starts with pocket money and spends a small amount on stationery.
scenarios.prompt: What two values matter, and how would you find the money left?
scenarios.sampleReasoning: Start with the original money, subtract the spent amount, and store what remains.

scenarios.title: Favorite Color List
scenarios.context: Three friends each say their favorite color.
scenarios.prompt: How would you keep all three colors together instead of making separate notes?
scenarios.sampleReasoning: Since all values are colors, I would keep them together in one list.

scenarios.title: First Item in a Bag
scenarios.context: A bag contains a pencil, eraser, and ruler in that order.
scenarios.prompt: How would you ask for only the first item?
scenarios.sampleReasoning: The first item is based on its position in the ordered group.

scenarios.title: Attendance Count
scenarios.context: A teacher has a short list of students present today.
scenarios.prompt: What question would help you find how many students are present?
scenarios.sampleReasoning: I would count how many names are in the present-students list.

scenarios.title: Temperature Message
scenarios.context: A weather display has one temperature reading.
scenarios.prompt: What simple rule would decide whether to show Hot or Comfortable?
scenarios.sampleReasoning: If the temperature is above the threshold, show Hot; otherwise show Comfortable.

scenarios.title: Water Bottle Reminder
scenarios.context: A learner wants a reminder for each break in the school day.
scenarios.prompt: How would you avoid writing the same reminder separately for every break?
scenarios.sampleReasoning: For every break, show the same water reminder.

scenarios.title: Find the Longest Pencil
scenarios.context: A learner has several pencil lengths and wants to know which one is longest.
scenarios.prompt: What small comparison would you repeat as you look through the lengths?
objectives: Compare one item at a time
scenarios.sampleReasoning: Start with one pencil as the longest, then compare each next pencil against it.

scenarios.title: Clean Chore Checklist
scenarios.context: A family has a checklist of small chores for Saturday morning.
scenarios.prompt: How would you go through each chore and mark it as done?
objectives: Process one chore at a time
scenarios.sampleReasoning: Put chores in a list and handle each chore one by one.

scenarios.title: Movie Age Filter
scenarios.context: A movie app has a list of films, each with a minimum age.
scenarios.prompt: What rule would decide which movies a 12-year-old can see?
scenarios.sampleReasoning: For each movie, keep it only if the learner age is at least the minimum age.

scenarios.title: Classroom Supply Lookup
scenarios.context: A class monitor tracks how many chalk pieces, markers, and notebooks are available.
scenarios.prompt: How would you store each supply name with its count?
scenarios.sampleReasoning: Each supply has a count, so I would store supply names as keys with counts as values.

scenarios.title: Bus Stop Search
scenarios.context: A route list contains several bus stops, and a learner wants to know if Library Stop is included.
scenarios.prompt: How would you check the stops one at a time until you find the target?
scenarios.sampleReasoning: Look at each stop and compare it with the stop I want.

scenarios.title: Average Practice Score
scenarios.context: A learner has five practice scores and wants one average score.
scenarios.prompt: What two small steps are needed before dividing?
scenarios.sampleReasoning: Find the total of all scores, count how many scores there are, then divide.

scenarios.title: Separate Even Roll Numbers
scenarios.context: A class list has roll numbers, and the teacher wants only even-numbered students for Team A.
scenarios.prompt: What small test tells you whether a roll number is even?
scenarios.sampleReasoning: A roll number is even if dividing by two leaves no remainder.

scenarios.title: Capitalize Name Tags
scenarios.context: A club has names typed in lowercase and wants neat name tags.
scenarios.prompt: What same text-cleaning action should happen to every name?
scenarios.sampleReasoning: For each name, convert it to title case before printing the tag.

scenarios.title: Find Missing Homework
scenarios.context: A teacher has one list of all students and another list of students who submitted homework.
scenarios.prompt: How would you reason about who is missing from the submitted list?
scenarios.sampleReasoning: Take everyone in the class and remove the students who submitted.

scenarios.title: Reusable Discount Rule
scenarios.context: A shop gives the same discount rule to many different bills.
scenarios.prompt: What inputs should a reusable discount helper receive?
scenarios.sampleReasoning: The helper needs the bill amount and should return the final price after applying the rule.

scenarios.title: Mini Quiz Checker
scenarios.context: A quiz app needs to check one answer against one correct answer.
scenarios.prompt: How would you design a tiny reusable checker for one question?
scenarios.sampleReasoning: Compare the learner answer with the correct answer and return whether they match.

scenarios.title: Step Counter Function
scenarios.context: A fitness note has a list of step counts from different walks.
scenarios.prompt: How would you make a reusable helper that returns the total steps?
scenarios.sampleReasoning: The function should take step counts, add them, and give back the total.

scenarios.title: Safe Username Maker
scenarios.context: A classroom app needs to turn a full name into a simple username.
scenarios.prompt: What small text transformations should a username function perform?
scenarios.sampleReasoning: Make the name lowercase and remove spaces so it can be used as a username.

scenarios.title: Retry Until Valid
scenarios.context: A form should keep asking until the learner enters a positive number.
scenarios.prompt: What condition tells the program to keep asking?
scenarios.sampleReasoning: Keep asking while the number is not positive, then stop once it is valid.

scenarios.title: Simple Score Report
scenarios.context: A tutor wants a small report showing a learner name and score together.
scenarios.prompt: How would you package two related pieces of information in one result?
scenarios.sampleReasoning: Return a dictionary with the learner name and score as labeled values.

scenarios.title: Task Status Updater
scenarios.context: A to-do app has tasks with statuses and needs to mark one task as done.
scenarios.prompt: What exact value changes when a task is completed?
scenarios.sampleReasoning: Find the task by name and change its status from pending to done.

scenarios.title: Small Receipt Builder
scenarios.context: A cafe wants a short text receipt for one item and its price.
scenarios.prompt: What pieces should a receipt function combine into a readable line?
scenarios.sampleReasoning: Combine the item name and price into one clear sentence.

scenarios.title: Choose Next Scenario
scenarios.context: PyBe wants to choose an easier or harder next scenario based on one score.
scenarios.prompt: What simple score rule could decide the next difficulty?
scenarios.sampleReasoning: If the score is high choose harder, if low choose easier, otherwise stay similar.

scenarios.title: Reflection Keyword Finder
scenarios.context: PyBe reads a short reflection and checks whether the learner mentioned confusion.
scenarios.prompt: What tiny search would detect that the reflection may need extra support?
scenarios.sampleReasoning: Look for words like confused or stuck and flag the reflection if they appear.

sessions.reasoning: I think if one scored atleast 10 then they should have harder level next time
sessions.promptText: Explain my approach step by step

abstractionMap.explanation: You are branching based on a condition, which is exactly what conditional statements express.
You are transforming values into a result, so variables and expressions become useful.

sessions.codeExplanation: The code starts from your natural reasoning and turns it into Python structure: Decision making becomes if / elif / else; Computation becomes variables and arithmetic expressions.
promptFeedback: Add more context about the situation and expected output.
Include an example input or output to make the prompt testable.
Name the Python concept you think may apply.
sessions.reflection: What did you notice about my thinking?
